title: The Macro Minute: Has the stock market fully priced a positive resolution to the Iran conflict?

Happy Friday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Dale, to
present our macro minute for Friday,
April 17th, 2026. As always, we start
with the executive summary from today's
lead-off morning note. So, let's dive
right in. Today's key macro question is,
has the stock market fully priced a
positive resolution to the
US-Israel-Iran conflict? Our answer is
likely, at least according to
incrementally bearish signals from our
crowding model. As indicated yesterday,
however, other elements of our
fundamental research summary and
quantitative risk management summary
that have longer forecasting horizons
continue to signal blue skies ahead for,
quote, dumb money bulls and storm clouds
ahead for, quote, smart money bears.
High turnover traders should sell the
rip. Thoughtful, dispassionate investors
should buy the dip. Eyes on the prize,
whereas prize equals our bullish active
themes, uh, starting with our resilient
US economy theme, which we introduced in
September of 2022 with a time horizon of
1 year plus from today, our bullish
paradigm C theme, uh, which we
introduced in April of last year with a
time horizon of 1 year plus from today,
our bullish productivity boom theme, uh,
which we introduced in October of last
year with a time horizon of 1 year plus
from today, our bullish jobless recovery
theme, which we also introduced in
October of last year with a time horizon
of 1 year plus from today, as well as
our cooling housing and labor theme, uh,
which we introduced in December of last
year with a time horizon of 1 year plus
from today. A lot of bullish stuff. Uh,
so, as always, we wrap up with the
question, uh, from our community here.
Uh, this one's titled, uh, is the stock
market underpricing an imminent global
oil shortage? It says, DD team 42, hello
Darius and friends. Hello to you. An
anecdote, my flight to Europe this
summer got canceled yesterday by Norse
Airways due to, quote, extraordinary
surge in oil prices followed by
unpredictable fuel supply shortage
constraints across the aviation industry
outside our control, end quote. I wonder
uh, who and where else are companies
pulling back on economic activity due to
fuel supply shortages? And is there an
oncoming wave of scaling back? And given
that the traffic distributor moves is
yet to rebound to pre-war levels, are we
in, uh, for supply shock? Uh, so, the
answer is very clearly yes, we're
already in a, uh, energy supply shock.
Uh, in our opinion, going to the
question, is the stock market
underpricing the imminent impact of a
global oil shortage? In our view, uh,
no, we think the market already priced
that in and now in the for the market
being the forward-looking beast that it
is, has already moving back to other
themes. Uh, good mainly our our bullish
active themes or other bullish themes.
But the key takeaway here in terms of my
our response to this question is,
there's always a full distribution of
probable economic, policy, and market
outcomes. Always, there's always a full
distribution and it's not our job to
determine whether or not the market is
underpricing this risk or underpricing
that that risk. It's our job to notice
when the market shifts from pricing in
one side of the distribution to the
other side of the distribution faster
than other investors, so that we can put
our buy orders ahead and ahead of their
buy orders and have their flows make us
rich instead of the other way around or
put our sell orders ahead of their sell
orders, so that their sell orders aren't
making us poor.
So, uh, noodle on that, think about that
over the weekend. We'll catch back here
on Monday. Cheers.
